Q1: I tried to keep a consistent study routine, usually carving out about three hours a day spread across the week. Being a visual learner, I'd organize my materials with color-coded notes and diagrams, which took extra time but helped things stick. Honestly though, with limited resources at home, I often had to work around when I could access good internet or find a quiet space. I kept a planner, but life has a way of throwing things off track.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. I aimed for regular morning sessions when my head was clearest, but deadlines would push me into late-night cramming more often than I'd like to admit. Working part-time and managing other responsibilities meant I couldn't always stick to my ideal schedule. Some weeks were structured, others were pure survival mode.
Q3: Usually it was checking for new assignments or announcements that got me to log in. I'd also log in when I knew a deadline was approaching and needed to double-check requirements. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about a discussion post or feedback, and that would pull me back in. Fear of missing something important was honestly a big motivator for me.
Q4: It took a few weeks, but eventually logging in became almost automatic, like checking my phone in the morning. I started associating certain times—like right after breakfast—with opening the LMS to see what was new. Having those 15 online courses meant there was always something happening, so the habit formed out of necessity. It became part of my daily rhythm, even if some days I just skimmed through quickly.
Q5: The video lectures were really valuable for me since I'm a visual learner—I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the diagrams and demonstrations. I also appreciated any materials that had clear visual layouts or infographics. The discussion forums were helpful when other students asked questions I hadn't thought of, which gave me different perspectives. Anything that let me see concepts rather than just read dense text worked best for my brain.
Q6: I'll admit I often skipped the longer reading materials, especially PDFs that were just walls of text without visuals. Some of the supplementary resources felt nice-to-have rather than essential, so when time was tight, they got pushed aside. I also didn't engage much with some of the optional quizzes because I was already stretched thin with the required work. It wasn't that they weren't useful—I just had to prioritize.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to assignments or exams. If I could tell it would help me understand something I was struggling with, I'd invest the effort. Visual demonstrations or worked examples made me feel like the time spent would actually pay off. Honestly, if something felt like busywork with no clear purpose, my motivation to complete it dropped significantly.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. Required ones had this weight to them—I knew they'd impact my grade, so even when I was exhausted, I'd push through. With optional stuff, I'd think 'that looks interesting' but then reality would hit and I'd prioritize what was mandatory. There was always this guilt about skipping optional content, but I had to be realistic about my bandwidth.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of materials to study offline because my internet at home isn't always reliable. I'd print diagrams and create handwritten visual notes that I'd review away from my computer. I also used YouTube videos and free resources to supplement what was in the course when I needed different explanations. A lot of my actual learning happened with physical notes spread across my desk, not logged into anything.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing printed materials or my own hand-drawn concept maps without touching the LMS. I'd study with a classmate in person, working through problems together, and none of that showed up anywhere. Sometimes I'd watch a video once online, then replay concepts in my head while commuting or before bed. The LMS only captured a fraction of the mental work I was putting in.
Q11: Yes, this was my standard approach actually. I'd download lecture slides and videos when I had good internet access, then study them repeatedly offline throughout the week. I'd watch a video once online to download it, then review it three or four more times on my laptop without being connected. This was essential for me given my internet situation, but it meant my actual engagement looked much lower than it was.
Q12: There were definitely moments when everything felt overwhelming and I'd freeze up a bit. Around midterms, when multiple deadlines converged and I was also dealing with financial stress, engaging with the course felt almost impossible. I'd feel scared about falling behind, which ironically made it harder to start working. Those CBT sessions I had helped me recognize these patterns and push through, but it wasn't easy.
Q13: All of those factors affected me at different points. Fatigue from working part-time while studying full-time meant some nights I'd log in but barely process anything. When I was confused about a topic, I'd sometimes avoid the LMS because facing the material felt daunting. Competing responsibilities—family stuff, work, life—constantly pulled my attention away from the course. I had to learn to work with these limitations rather than pretend they didn't exist.
Q14: Honestly, yes. There were times I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through materials, but my mind was elsewhere—worrying about bills or just mentally exhausted. I'd watch a video and realize at the end that I hadn't absorbed anything. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actually learning. It's frustrating because the time was spent, but the benefit wasn't there.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments even when I knew they mattered. Sometimes the fear of not doing well enough made me avoid starting—like if I didn't try, I couldn't fail. Other times I was just so overwhelmed that I'd convince myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt more capable. The CBT work helped me understand this avoidance pattern, but breaking it was still a daily struggle. I'd often end up cramming at the last minute because of this.
Q16: Deadlines basically dictated my LMS usage. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active—downloading materials, rewatching videos, checking rubrics multiple times. My login frequency spiked around due dates and dropped between them. It wasn't ideal, but that external pressure was often what I needed to overcome my avoidance tendencies. The structure of deadlines gave me something concrete to work toward.
Q17: Feedback was really important to me, even though waiting for it made me anxious. When I got positive feedback, it motivated me to engage more with similar activities. Constructive criticism sometimes stung initially, but it helped me focus my efforts better. I'd often revisit the LMS specifically to check for feedback, and it shaped how I approached subsequent assignments. Knowing someone was actually looking at my work made it feel more worthwhile.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework I desperately needed, even if I didn't always follow it perfectly. Having clear modules and deadlines helped me break down what felt like an overwhelming amount of content. When the structure was logical and built on previous material, I could see my progress. But sometimes having 15 courses meant the structures conflicted, and I'd have to make tough choices about where to focus my limited energy.
Q19: Visual elements in the course design made engagement much easier for me—clear layouts, diagrams, video content. When materials were text-heavy or poorly organized, I'd struggle to stay focused and would procrastinate more. Courses that broke content into smaller chunks were more manageable than those with massive single uploads. The discussion forums were helpful when they were active, but sometimes they felt empty and that made engagement feel pointless.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying hard but struggling to complete work to a standard they're satisfied with. They might be researching, reviewing, starting and restarting assignments without finishing. Fear of failure or perfectionism could keep them active but not productive. Or maybe they're dealing with personal issues that consume their mental energy even while they're technically present in the system. Activity doesn't equal output.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. I did a lot of studying offline that wouldn't show in my logs, but I still managed to earn Merit. Someone might have efficient study strategies or strong background knowledge that lets them learn quickly without extensive LMS time. They might also be using external resources or study groups that don't get captured. Low activity doesn't necessarily mean low effort or low learning.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend on pages. They're good at capturing deadline-driven behavior and basic engagement metrics. But they miss the quality of attention, offline studying, emotional state, and whether someone actually understood what they were looking at. They can't see the hours I spent with printed notes or the mental processing that happened away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, making connections between concepts. For me, engagement looks like creating visual summaries, discussing ideas with peers, and thinking about the content even when I'm not studying. It's mental and emotional investment, not just time logged. You can be physically present but mentally checked out, and that's not real engagement.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I felt sometimes, or how much emotional energy it took to push through that fear. It wouldn't capture the CBT strategies I used to manage my anxiety about assignments, or the offline support systems that kept me going. It can't see that I'm working with limited resources and juggling multiple responsibilities. My resilience—showing up despite everything—doesn't translate into data points, but it's the most important part of my story.
